topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The name hung in the air like a misplaced chord: Ms. Eleanor Vance. I stared at the faded photograph on the mantelpiece, the one from my own early years, and then down at my son, Leo, who was currently attempting to build a precarious tower out of wooden blocks on the floor. His brow was furrowed in concentration, the exact same slight arch of the left eyebrow I remembered from my own childhood. A cold, hollow feeling settled in my stomach, a sudden drop in temperature that had nothing to do with the drafty window. I walked over to Leo, my movements stiff, and knelt beside him. "Leo," I began, my voice sounding strangely thin, "do you remember Ms. Vance?" He looked up, his eyes wide and unreadable, and for a fleeting second, I saw a flicker of something—recognition, perhaps, or maybe just blankness. The silence stretched between us, heavy and suffocating, making the small sounds of the house—the ticking clock, the distant traffic—seem overwhelmingly loud.

[story 2]

It happened over lukewarm coffee. I was flipping through Leo’s school report card, a routine chore that usually offered a comforting predictability. Then I saw it: Teacher: Ms. Eleanor Vance. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that caught in my chest. I dropped the paper onto the table with a clatter that echoed in the quiet kitchen. My hands started to tremble, the edges of the paper blurring. I looked at Leo, who was humming tunelessly while stacking cereal bowls. He looked up, and his gaze met mine. There was a strange stillness in his eyes, a vacant quality that made my throat tighten. I couldn't form a coherent sentence. "Leo," I managed, the word emerging as a dry rasp. He simply blinked, a slow, deliberate movement, and then turned back to his bowls, completely unperturbed by the seismic shift that had just occurred in the room.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the sequence in my head: the classroom, the scent of chalk dust and old paper, the way Ms. Vance always corrected grammar with a precise, almost unnerving calm. Now, seeing that familiar silhouette in my son’s face, the world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis. I stood by the hallway mirror, watching myself. My reflection looked unfamiliar, my features stretched taut with an unidentifiable tension. I reached out, touching my cheek, feeling the skin beneath my fingertips as if it belonged to someone else. When Leo came into the room, carrying a backpack, he paused at the doorway. He didn't smile, didn't frown; he just observed me with an intense, steady focus that stripped away any sense of familiarity. The air felt thick, pressurized, and I found myself unable to draw a full breath.
